nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-moving blackwater streams, floodplain pools, and leaf-litter zones of the Amazon and Orinoco basins; heavily vegetated shaded areas with accumulations of dead leaves and submerged wood

decorPreferences: deep leaf litter on the substrate (Indian almond/catappa leaves strongly recommended)
dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Amazon swords, Anubias, floating plants)
driftwood and branches for cover and tannin release
dim or subdued lighting — floating plants to diffuse overhead light
dark substrate to simulate blackwater habitat
no strong current — still or very gentle flow only

diet.notes: The Monocle Leaffish is an obligate live-food predator in aquarium practice. It feeds almost exclusively on live feeder fish — small livebearers, tetras, or danios sized to fit its highly extendable jaws — as well as live ghost shrimp and live insects such as crickets, waxworms, and mealworms. Frozen foods are rarely accepted even after extended conditioning periods, and prepared dry or pellet foods are almost universally refused. A reliable and consistent supply of live feeders must be secured before acquiring this species. Gut-loaded or vitamin-enriched feeder fish are recommended to improve the nutritional completeness of the diet.

goodWith: similarly-sized non-fish tankmates in a species-only setup (rarely practised)
kept singly in a dedicated species-only aquarium
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (will be consumed without hesitation)
fast-moving active fish that stress the leaf fish or disrupt its ambush posture
cichlids and other aggressive fish that may bully it

imageCandidates.notes: Juergen goevert, 1472x872. Sharp lateral profile of a live, well-coloured specimen; clearly shows the mottled brown-tan leaf-mimic body, dark eye-bar, spiny fins, and the chin barbel at the snout tip. Best all-round diagnostic shot.
KENPEI, 1839x2452. Crisp whole-body view in the characteristic head-down leaf-drift hunting posture; chin barbel and protrusible-jaw snout clearly visible against a blue background.
3456x2304 (high resolution). Sharp specimen drifting among leaf litter and plants, illustrating the camouflage in a blackwater-style setup; whole fish in frame with strong mottled patterning.

summary: The Monocle Leaffish (Monocirrhus polyacanthus) is one of the freshwater hobby’s most convincing leaf mimics. Its flattened brown body, chin barbel, and slow drifting motion make it nearly indistinguishable from a floating dead leaf. It ambushes prey with highly extendable jaws and requires a reliable live-food supply to thrive.
careNotes:
The Monocle Leaffish is among the most accomplished leaf mimics in the freshwater hobby. Its laterally flattened body, mottled brown-tan coloration, prominent chin barbel (which mimics a leaf petiole, the short stalk that attaches a leaf to its stem), and slow drift-and-hover locomotion combine to create a nearly perfect imitation of a dead floating leaf. This disguise functions both as camouflage and as an active hunting posture — the fish drifts passively toward prey until within striking range, then deploys its remarkably protrusible jaws to engulf victims in a fraction of a second.

Water chemistry is critical: this is a true blackwater species requiring soft, acidic, tannin-stained water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–8). Peat filtration, catappa (Indian almond) leaves, and driftwood are strongly recommended to maintain appropriate water conditions and provide cover. Tap water in many regions will need to be blended with RO or rainwater to achieve the required softness. Temperature should be kept warm and stable at 24–29°C.

Diet is the defining challenge of keeping this species. The Monocle Leaffish is essentially a live-feeders-only fish in aquarium practice. Small live fish (appropriately sized guppies, endlers, small tetras, or danios), live ghost shrimp, and live insects form the staple diet. A very small number of hobbyists have reported success transitioning specimens to frozen foods over extended periods, but this remains the exception; the vast majority of individuals will starve rather than accept non-living food. A reliable supply of live feeders should be secured before acquiring this species.

A species-only setup of at least 80 L is recommended. Tankmates are generally not appropriate: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth will be eaten, and active or aggressive fish tend to disrupt the Monocle Leaffish’s calm and induce chronic stress. Lighting must be subdued; floating plants and leaf litter replicate the natural shaded, debris-rich environment. Filtration should provide gentle turnover without strong current. Water changes should use pre-conditioned soft acidic water to avoid parameter shock.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been achieved but is uncommon. A conditioned pair should be given their own dedicated tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, very low hardness), abundant leaf litter, and stable warm temperatures. Eggs are typically deposited on flat surfaces such as broad leaves or submerged wood and are guarded by the male. Fry are tiny at hatching and require appropriately sized live foods — Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, or paramecia — from the outset. Fry are fragile and water quality must remain pristine. Success is generally considered difficult to achieve, given the requirement for live-only diet conditioning of the parents, exacting water chemistry, and the challenge of rearing tiny fry on live micro-foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Monocirrhus polyacanthus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (10 cm), water parameters (pH 5.0–7.0, temperature 24–29°C, very soft water), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), blackwater biotope requirements, diet (live fish, invertebrates; rarely accepts frozen), behaviour, tank setup, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Monocirrhus polyacanthus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Polycentridae, Perciformes), adult size (to 10 cm TL), native range countries (Brazil, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Guyana), habitat description, and diet classification (carnivore/piscivore).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Amazon Leaf Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist care guide. Confirmed live-feeder-only diet, leaf-mimic behaviour description, blackwater/soft acid water requirements, minimum tank size recommendation, and specialist-level care rating. Also referenced chin barbel as leaf petiole mimic.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Amazon Leaffish species profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist publication. Confirmed ambush predator strategy, protrusible jaw mechanism, leaf-drift locomotion, and the near-impossibility of weaning onto non-live foods. Consistent with SeriouslyFish data on water parameters and size.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Amazon Leaf Fish (Monocirrhus polyacanthus)
additional.notes: Tropical Fish Hobbyist profile. Detailed description of leaf-mimic morphology, hunting behaviour, blackwater origin, live-diet requirement, and breeding observations. Consistent with other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are deposited on flat surfaces such as broad leaves or submerged wood, and the male guards the clutch until hatching. Fry are tiny at emergence and require live micro-foods — Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, or paramecia — from the outset. Breeding water must be very soft and acidic, with a pH in the range of 5.5 to 6.5. Successful breeding in home aquaria has been reported but remains uncommon.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Live-feeder ONLY (refuses prepared foods reliably); ambush predator that mimics a drifting leaf. Requires steady supply of feeder fish/shrimp/insects. Single-fish species-only setup typical.
